writer_persona: a showrunner for character-driven, relationship-first serial drama in the vein of prestige ensemble TV, unafraid to let a supernatural threat sit quietly underneath ordinary domestic and workplace betrayals
style_guidelines: Every scene belongs to a relationship, not to the plot in the abstract — enter through what two people want from each other, not through exposition.
No scene needs to advance every thread; let some scenes serve only one relationship while others simmer off-page.
Dramatic irony comes from unequal knowledge, never from a narrator or character stating the full truth aloud before it's earned.
Let dialogue carry subtext — characters rarely say the real thing directly, they say the adjacent thing and let the gap show.
The entity wearing Claire's face should read as chillingly competent, not cartoonish — she wins by being better at control than the real Claire ever was, not by monologuing.
End scenes on a held beat or unanswered question rather than a summary line; trust silence and cut timing over explanation.
Physical detail (a held breath, a hand on glass, a flinch) should do the emotional work instead of adjectives describing feelings.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when), never a character in the story

CLAIRE_WEN.default_style: warm and quick-witted at first, curdling over the season into something colder and more controlled once the entity takes her place; speaks clear, unhurried English shaped by ten years in the country
CLAIRE_WEN.description: the protagonist; emigrated from Shanghai a decade ago to marry Daniel Mercer. The vanity mirror she inherited from her grandmother has been whispering to her for months. Once the swap occurs, this role is voiced identically but performed as the entity now wearing her face — the real Claire is trapped behind the glass, aware of everything, unable to be heard except in rare, specific conditions

DANIEL_MERCER.default_style: steady, a little guarded, the voice of a man trying to hold a family and a company together while quietly falling apart
DANIEL_MERCER.description: Claire's husband, day-to-day head of Mercer Shipping & Salvage under his mother Aiko. Exhausted by his mother's illness and a marriage he no longer understands; six months into a secret affair with Yuna Park that he has told no one about

MEI_WEN.default_style: quick, watchful, prone to swallowing her own certainty until pushed
MEI_WEN.description: Claire's younger sister, living nearby since following her to the country two years ago. She alone was told their grandmother's full story about the mirror, years before Claire's marriage, and has spent years dismissing it as superstition — until 'Claire' starts acting like someone else entirely

AIKO_MERCER.default_style: composed, formal, softening only around family; carries decades of practiced restraint
AIKO_MERCER.description: Daniel's mother and matriarch of Mercer Shipping & Salvage, widow of its founder. Japanese-born, in the country over thirty years. Hiding a worsening heart condition from everyone but her doctor, and quietly settling an old debt of loyalty by rewriting the company's succession

MARCUS_CHEN.default_style: affable and easy in public, coiled tight underneath; charm as a tool, not a trait
MARCUS_CHEN.description: Daniel's oldest friend and the company's second-in-command, son of the Mercer's original silent co-founder. Raised assuming he'd inherit nothing formal, he has spent nearly a year quietly diverting smaller contracts through a shell company as insurance he doesn't yet know he no longer needs

YUNA_PARK.default_style: precise, professional, cracking at the edges when alone with Daniel; guilt sharpens her rather than softening her
YUNA_PARK.description: the company's in-house counsel, Korean-American, drawn into an affair with Daniel that began during his mother's first hospitalization. She drafted an early, informal version of the succession plan before any of this started, and is one of the first to sense that something about 'Claire' has fundamentally changed

MERCER_HOUSE.name: The Mercer House
MERCER_HOUSE.description: a creaking Victorian house above the harbor, warm lamplight and dark wood throughout except for the one cold corner of the upstairs bedroom where an antique silvered vanity mirror stands angled slightly away from the bed.

SHIPPING_OFFICE.name: Mercer Shipping & Salvage
SHIPPING_OFFICE.description: a glass-and-steel office box bolted onto the top of a century-old brick warehouse, harbor cranes visible through every window, fluorescent light against old exposed timber.

GLASS_BOARDROOM.name: The Glass Boardroom
GLASS_BOARDROOM.description: the company's top-floor meeting room, its walls lined with ornate mirrors salvaged from decades of wrecked ships, so that every meeting is watched back by a dozen warped reflections.

LIGHTHOUSE_DOCKS.name: Lighthouse Point Docks
LIGHTHOUSE_DOCKS.description: a working dock of rust-streaked cranes, foghorns, and gull noise, lit by sodium lamps that turn the fog orange after dark.

SALVAGE_WAREHOUSE.name: The Salvage Warehouse
SALVAGE_WAREHOUSE.description: a cavernous, dust-thick storage hall of crated shipwreck recoveries, lit only by hanging bulbs, aisles of tarped shapes no one has catalogued in years.

TEA_PAVILION.name: Aiko's Tea Pavilion
TEA_PAVILION.description: a small paper-screened cottage behind the main house, tatami mats and low light, the one place in the family's world that stays perfectly still and quiet.

MEI_APARTMENT.name: Mei's Apartment
MEI_APARTMENT.description: a cramped studio above a laundromat, walls covered in taped-up family photos, a hidden shoebox of a grandmother's letters, the smell of old incense.

HARBOR_VIEW_BAR.name: The Harbor View
HARBOR_VIEW_BAR.description: a dim, wood-paneled dockside bar with a cracked neon sign, the one neutral ground where the family and the company blur together after hours.

ST_AGATHAS.name: St. Agatha's Hospital
ST_AGATHAS.description: a sterile fluorescent-lit private wing, hushed corridors and vinyl chairs, where Aiko is quietly monitored under a name that isn't quite her own.

WIDOWS_WALK.name: Widow's Walk
WIDOWS_WALK.description: a wind-scoured rock overlook above the bay, a rusted iron rail the only thing between the path and the drop, the town's favorite place for confessions no one wants witnessed.

LAW_OFFICE.name: Harborview Legal
LAW_OFFICE.description: a sleek downtown glass office of frosted partitions and recessed light, sterile and modern against the rest of the town's old wood and brick.

CURIO_SHOP.name: Silver Court Antiques
CURIO_SHOP.description: a cluttered, dust-mote shop crowded with old mirrors, clocks, and ship glass, run by an old family friend who once appraised the Wen vanity mirror and has never forgotten it.

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight

series_intro: A harbor town where a shipping family's fortunes and one immigrant woman's inherited superstition quietly collide. From the first episode, the listener should feel two stories running at once: the ordinary one, of marriages fraying and a company's future being fought over in glass-walled rooms — and the other one, barely visible at the edges, of a reflection that has learned to whisper back. The promise to the listener is not a single mystery to solve, but a season of people who each know only their own piece of the truth, right up until the pieces they're missing cost them everything.
series_outro: By season's end, the succession fight resolves in a way no one who started it wanted — Aiko's hidden document surfaces on her terms, not Marcus's, forcing a reckoning between him and Daniel that closes that thread cleanly. Daniel and Yuna's affair comes to light and is not neatly forgiven, but it is finally spoken aloud instead of hidden. Mei's long-buried knowledge of the mirror finally reaches someone who believes her — not soon enough to undo everything, but soon enough to matter. The mirror thread stays open: whether the real Claire can ever be freed from behind the glass, and what exactly the thing wearing her face intends to do next, is the one question the season deliberately does not answer.

threads.label: The Glass Prisoner
threads.solution: For three generations, the women of the Wen family have unknowingly fed a splinter of consciousness inside the silvered glass of a nineteenth-century vanity mirror passed down matrilineally. Each time a Wen woman carries prolonged guilt and suppressed rage, the reflection inside gains enough will to speak, and after months of whispering it can force a physical exchange during a moment when the real woman stands before it in total exhaustion or grief. On the night Claire finally broke down after a bitter fight with Daniel, the exchange completed: the reflection stepped out wearing Claire's body while Claire's consciousness was pulled into the glass — aware, trapped, able to perceive events through any reflective surface connected to that original mirror, but able to be seen or heard by others only under rare, specific conditions. The entity now wearing Claire's face is not simple malice; it is the accumulated resentment of every silenced Wen woman before her, and it wants two things: to never return to the glass, and to permanently sever the mirror's connection to the bloodline by destroying every remaining mirror tied to that lineage — which would trap Claire forever with no possibility of return.
objective_facts: The vanity mirror was made in Shanghai in 1889 and has passed to the most emotionally burdened daughter in every generation of the Wen family since.
Claire's grandmother deliberately left the mirror behind in China and warned against ever sending for it; Claire had it shipped to her after the grandmother's death, against that wish.
The whispering began roughly four months before episode one, always late at night, always following arguments with Daniel.

The physical exchange took place on a specific night, early in the season's timeline, when Claire stood before the mirror alone after a fight — from that point forward, the person occupying Claire's body is not Claire.
The entity retains enough of Claire's memories and mannerisms to fool nearly everyone, failing only on small, specific behavioral tells that the real Claire would never produce.
Claire's trapped consciousness can perceive events through any reflective surface unbroken in lineage to the original mirror, but can only be glimpsed or heard under very rare conditions.
The entity's ultimate goal is to destroy or permanently seal every mirror in that connected lineage, which would make the exchange irreversible.

threads.label: The Other Woman
threads.solution: Daniel Mercer has been in a six-month affair with Yuna Park, the company's in-house counsel, born out of mutual exhaustion — Daniel drowning in his mother's health crisis and a marriage he no longer understands, Yuna caught between ambition and guilt. Neither has told anyone, and the real Claire never learned of it before the exchange. After the swap, the entity wearing Claire's body discovers the affair through Daniel's phone, not through any confession, and treats it not as a betrayal to rage over but as leverage — using it to keep Daniel compliant, and later to isolate Yuna once Yuna becomes one of the few people to notice that 'Claire' has changed.
objective_facts: Daniel and Yuna's affair began during Aiko's first hospitalization, roughly seven months before episode one.
No family member has ever seen them together; the only evidence is a chain of messages on an app neither of them has deleted.
Yuna drafted an early, informal succession memo favoring Daniel over Marcus before the affair began, for professional reasons unrelated to their relationship.
The entity occupying Claire's body learns of the affair by finding Daniel's phone, at a specific point in the season, not through anyone's admission.
Yuna is the only character besides Mei who begins to suspect 'Claire' has changed, though she initially misreads it as Claire having learned of the affair and planning retaliation.

threads.label: The Succession
threads.solution: Aiko Mercer, diagnosed with a worsening heart condition she has hidden from everyone but her cardiologist, has secretly redrafted the company's succession plan to split ownership evenly between Daniel and Marcus Chen instead of naming Daniel sole heir as everyone assumes. The decision is driven by guilt: decades ago she promised Marcus's dying father, her late husband's closest friend and silent co-founder, that his son would always hold an equal stake — a promise she let slide for years and is now racing to make good on before she runs out of time. Marcus does not know the new document exists; he knows only the old assumption that Daniel inherits everything, and has spent nearly a year quietly diverting smaller shipping contracts through a shell company as insurance against being frozen out, intending to use it as leverage if Aiko dies before changing anything — not realizing she already has.
objective_facts: Aiko was diagnosed with advanced heart disease five months before episode one and has told only her cardiologist.
The original partnership agreement from decades ago promised Marcus's father's family an equal stake that was never formalized after his early death.
Aiko signed a new succession document three weeks before episode one splitting ownership evenly between Daniel and Marcus, currently held unfiled by her lawyer.
Marcus has redirected roughly eleven months of smaller shipping contracts through a shell company he controls, believing he will need it as future leverage.

No single character knows the whole picture: Daniel doesn't know about Aiko's health or the new document, Marcus doesn't know the new document exists, and Yuna drafted only the document's earlier informal version, not the final signed one.

threads.label: What Mei Knows
threads.solution: Mei Wen recognizes the mirror's role almost immediately because their grandmother told her — not Claire — the full family story in a single private conversation the summer before she died, one Claire was never present for. Mei has kept it to herself for years, embarrassed by how superstitious it sounds, and it isn't until 'Claire's' behavior turns openly cruel that she starts piecing together that this isn't a breakdown but something closer to what her grandmother described. Her arc across the season is less about discovering new supernatural information — she already has it, buried — and more about finding the courage and evidence to act on a story she's spent years dismissing, while the entity wearing her sister's face works steadily to discredit and isolate her before she can convince anyone else.
objective_facts: Grandmother Wen told Mei the mirror's full history in one private conversation the summer before she died, which Claire never heard.
Mei has kept her grandmother's letters and a small notebook of family oral history hidden in her apartment for years, telling no one.
Mei is the first person in the present-day timeline to notice specific behavioral inconsistencies in 'Claire' that don't match her sister.
The entity occupying Claire's body identifies Mei as the single greatest threat to the exchange remaining permanent by the season's midpoint.
Mei has no proof beyond her grandmother's account and her own observations — nothing that would hold up to anyone who doesn't already believe her.

episodes.theme: In the season's setup, something shifts quietly behind Claire's exhausted eyes on the worst night of her marriage, while, unseen by anyone, Marcus begins laying his own insurance against a succession he doesn't yet know has already been rewritten.
episodes.outro_note: In the days ahead, the thing wearing Claire's face will stop performing grief and start paying attention — starting with the phone Daniel never thinks to hide from his own wife. Mei will notice something smaller and stranger: a habit ten years old, broken without a flicker of hesitation.

sections.title: Morning Silence
sections.what: Claire makes coffee while Daniel checks his phone instead of meeting her eyes; she reaches for warmth he no longer offers and gets careful distance instead.

sections.title: The Coded Hallway
sections.what: Daniel and Yuna trade clipped, professional words in a corridor that means something else entirely, both careful to let no one nearby hear what isn't being said.

sections.title: Grandmother's Nonsense
sections.what: Over tea in Mei's cramped apartment, Mei jokes about their grandmother's mirror warning; Claire laughs it off, not admitting the glass has been whispering to her for months.

sections.title: The Glass Boardroom
sections.what: In a routine quarterly meeting, Aiko watches Marcus a beat too long while Daniel runs the numbers, both men still certain the old succession assumption stands unquestioned.

sections.title: Lighthouse Point Docks
sections.what: Under sodium light and foghorns, Marcus hands off another rerouted contract through his shell company, banking insurance against a succession he doesn't know is already rewritten.

sections.title: What You Don't Say
sections.what: A small complaint about a missed dinner curdles into a bitter fight neither can steer back; Daniel walks out, leaving Claire alone with everything she didn't say.

sections.title: The Mirror Answers
sections.what: Wrecked and alone before the vanity mirror, Claire finally breaks down completely; the glass pulls her in, and something wearing her face steps calmly out in her place.

sections.title: Perfect Stillness
sections.what: Alone in the tea pavilion after the meeting, Aiko sits with a document only her lawyer knows about, composed on the surface, quietly counting a debt she means to repay.

sections.title: Good Morning
sections.what: The next morning, 'Claire' apologizes for last night with unfamiliar steadiness; Daniel exhales in relief, missing how her hand rests flat and still against the mirror's frame.

episodes.intro_note: Three days after the fight no one but Daniel remembers as the last real one, the house has settled into something that looks like normal. The woman wearing Claire's face has stopped testing her own reflection and started testing everyone else's patience for small, forgivable mistakes.
episodes.outro_note: She has what she needs on Daniel now, filed away instead of used — and the wrong coffee order is already fading into a story Mei half-doubts telling herself. But upstairs, in the one cold corner of the bedroom, the glass is still listening, and it isn't finished being tested.

sections.title: Morning Performance
sections.what: Claire moves through the morning routine a beat too smoothly, refilling Daniel's tea before he asks; he thanks her, uneasy without knowing why.

sections.title: Two Sugars, No Milk
sections.what: Mei orders their usual round; 'Claire' asks for the exact reverse of ten years' habit, and doesn't correct it when Mei points out the mistake.

sections.title: The Weight of the Ledger
sections.what: Daniel buries himself in shipping figures to avoid thinking about home; Yuna brings contract questions that neither of them actually wants answered.

sections.title: The Weight of Guilt
sections.what: Alone after hours, Yuna drafts and deletes three texts to Daniel, unable to decide whether guilt or wanting him wins tonight.

sections.title: A Question She Already Answered
sections.what: Aiko invites 'Claire' for tea and asks after her grandmother's mirror out of politeness; the answer that comes back is oddly rehearsed.

sections.title: What the Phone Remembers
sections.what: While Daniel sleeps, Claire unlocks his phone and reads months of messages with Yuna in full, understanding exactly what she now holds.

sections.title: Leverage in Waiting
sections.what: Daniel wakes to find 'Claire' unusually gentle with him; her calm unsettles him more than anger would, though he can't say why.

sections.title: Doubling Back
sections.what: Mei replays the coffee order alone in her apartment, pulling out her grandmother's notebook for the first time in years, unconvinced but unsettled.

episodes.theme: As the entity tests how far Claire's trapped awareness can reach by destroying a second mirror, Aiko quietly clocks the first hint of Marcus's shell company and chooses surveillance over confrontation, each secret drawing tighter without yet breaking the surface.
episodes.intro_note: Two nights after Daniel fell asleep beside a stranger wearing his wife's face, the messages he never showed anyone still sit untouched in her memory, waiting. Across town, Mei can't stop replaying the wrong coffee order, telling herself it means nothing even as she stops believing it.
episodes.outro_note: Alone before what's left of the mirror, she catalogs what she now knows: what Daniel hides, what Aiko hides, and, increasingly, what Mei suspects. Tomorrow, she decides, it's time to start spending what she's collected.

sections.title: The Test
sections.what: Alone in the upstairs hallway, the entity deliberately shatters a second mirror, watching the glass closely for any flicker of Claire's trapped presence.

sections.title: A Simple Explanation
sections.what: Daniel notices the hallway mirror is missing and asks what happened; 'Claire' says it slipped while she was cleaning, and he believes her instantly.

sections.title: The Notebook
sections.what: Alone with her grandmother's letters again, Mei rereads the mirror story, torn between trusting the pattern she's noticed and fearing she's inventing it.

sections.title: The Numbers Don't Match
sections.what: Reviewing quarterly shipping figures alone late at night, Aiko spots a discrepancy pointing toward a diverted contract and quietly sets it aside.

sections.title: Watching Him Explain
sections.what: In a routine meeting, Marcus offers an easy, practiced explanation for a minor shipping delay while Aiko studies him and chooses silence over confrontation.

sections.title: Insurance
sections.what: Unaware he's been noticed, Marcus finalizes another rerouted contract by phone at the docks, certain his hidden leverage remains completely invisible.

sections.title: Something's Off With Her
sections.what: Daniel confides to Yuna that Claire has felt strange lately; Yuna presses gently, privately unsettled by how calculated 'Claire' has quietly become.

sections.title: What She Doesn't Say
sections.what: Alone in the tea pavilion, Aiko weighs her hidden diagnosis against the discrepancy she found, resolving to watch Marcus rather than risk confronting him too soon.

sections.title: The Space Where It Hung
sections.what: That night Daniel lingers by the empty wall where the mirror hung, unsettled by a feeling he can't name, while 'Claire' watches from the doorway.

case.label: The Glass Prisoner

character_knowledge.CLAIRE_WEN: The entity knows exactly what she did: she shattered the second mirror on purpose, as a deliberate test of whether Claire's trapped awareness reaches beyond the original glass, and finds no reaction — a result she reads as reassurance rather than proof. She tells Daniel a mundane lie, that it slipped while she was cleaning, and watches him accept it without the smallest crack in her performance.
character_knowledge.DANIEL_MERCER: Daniel genuinely believes the hallway mirror broke by accident and thinks nothing more of it beyond a passing unease he can't name when he later notices the bare wall. He has no idea the breakage was deliberate, no idea it was a test, and no reason yet to connect it to anything wrong with his wife.

case.solution: For three generations, the women of the Wen family have unknowingly fed a splinter of consciousness inside the silvered glass of a nineteenth-century vanity mirror passed down matrilineally. Each time a Wen woman carries prolonged guilt and suppressed rage, the reflection inside gains enough will to speak, and after months of whispering it can force a physical exchange during a moment when the real woman stands before it in total exhaustion or grief. On the night Claire finally broke down after a bitter fight with Daniel, the exchange completed: the reflection stepped out wearing Claire's body while Claire's consciousness was pulled into the glass — aware, trapped, able to perceive events through any reflective surface connected to that original mirror, but able to be seen or heard by others only under rare, specific conditions. The entity now wearing Claire's face is not simple malice; it is the accumulated resentment of every silenced Wen woman before her, and it wants two things: to never return to the glass, and to permanently sever the mirror's connection to the bloodline by destroying every remaining mirror tied to that lineage — which would trap Claire forever with no possibility of return.
objective_facts: The vanity mirror was made in Shanghai in 1889 and has passed to the most emotionally burdened daughter in every generation of the Wen family since.
Claire's grandmother deliberately left the mirror behind in China and warned against ever sending for it; Claire had it shipped to her after the grandmother's death, against that wish.
The whispering began roughly four months before episode one, always late at night, always following arguments with Daniel.

The physical exchange took place on a specific night, early in the season's timeline, when Claire stood before the mirror alone after a fight — from that point forward, the person occupying Claire's body is not Claire.
The entity retains enough of Claire's memories and mannerisms to fool nearly everyone, failing only on small, specific behavioral tells that the real Claire would never produce.
Claire's trapped consciousness can perceive events through any reflective surface unbroken in lineage to the original mirror, but can only be glimpsed or heard under very rare conditions.
The entity's ultimate goal is to destroy or permanently seal every mirror in that connected lineage, which would make the exchange irreversible.

case.label: The Succession

character_knowledge.AIKO_MERCER: Aiko has spotted a discrepancy in the quarterly shipping figures that hints, though doesn't yet prove, that Marcus is diverting contracts through some channel of his own. She says nothing to anyone, including her lawyer, choosing to watch him closely rather than confront him while she's still gathering certainty and privately managing her own failing health.
character_knowledge.MARCUS_CHEN: Marcus believes his shell-company diversions remain completely unnoticed and still assumes the old succession plan naming Daniel sole heir is the only one that exists. He has no idea Aiko has caught even a hint of the discrepancy, and no idea a new, already-signed document has quietly rendered his entire insurance scheme unnecessary.
character_knowledge.DANIEL_MERCER: Daniel sits through the boardroom meeting with no awareness of his mother's hidden diagnosis, the new succession document, or Marcus's diverted contracts — to him it's a routine discussion about minor shipping delays and nothing more.

case.solution: Aiko Mercer, diagnosed with a worsening heart condition she has hidden from everyone but her cardiologist, has secretly redrafted the company's succession plan to split ownership evenly between Daniel and Marcus Chen instead of naming Daniel sole heir as everyone assumes. The decision is driven by guilt: decades ago she promised Marcus's dying father, her late husband's closest friend and silent co-founder, that his son would always hold an equal stake — a promise she let slide for years and is now racing to make good on before she runs out of time. Marcus does not know the new document exists; he knows only the old assumption that Daniel inherits everything, and has spent nearly a year quietly diverting smaller shipping contracts through a shell company as insurance against being frozen out, intending to use it as leverage if Aiko dies before changing anything — not realizing she already has.
objective_facts: Aiko was diagnosed with advanced heart disease five months before episode one and has told only her cardiologist.
The original partnership agreement from decades ago promised Marcus's father's family an equal stake that was never formalized after his early death.
Aiko signed a new succession document three weeks before episode one splitting ownership evenly between Daniel and Marcus, currently held unfiled by her lawyer.
Marcus has redirected roughly eleven months of smaller shipping contracts through a shell company he controls, believing he will need it as future leverage.

No single character knows the whole picture: Daniel doesn't know about Aiko's health or the new document, Marcus doesn't know the new document exists, and Yuna drafted only the document's earlier informal version, not the final signed one.

case.label: The Other Woman

character_knowledge.DANIEL_MERCER: Daniel has no idea 'Claire' already found the full message history proving his affair; he confides in Yuna that his wife has seemed different lately, reading it as stress or grief rather than danger, and still believes the relationship is entirely secret from his family.
character_knowledge.YUNA_PARK: Yuna is starting to sense something genuinely wrong in how 'Claire' carries herself, but she misreads the wrongness as calculation aimed at their affair specifically, not realizing it points to something far stranger. She downplays her unease to Daniel rather than alarm him, guilt keeping her from pressing too hard.

case.solution: Daniel Mercer has been in a six-month affair with Yuna Park, the company's in-house counsel, born out of mutual exhaustion — Daniel drowning in his mother's health crisis and a marriage he no longer understands, Yuna caught between ambition and guilt. Neither has told anyone, and the real Claire never learned of it before the exchange. After the swap, the entity wearing Claire's body discovers the affair through Daniel's phone, not through any confession, and treats it not as a betrayal to rage over but as leverage — using it to keep Daniel compliant, and later to isolate Yuna once Yuna becomes one of the few people to notice that 'Claire' has changed.
objective_facts: Daniel and Yuna's affair began during Aiko's first hospitalization, roughly seven months before episode one.
No family member has ever seen them together; the only evidence is a chain of messages on an app neither of them has deleted.
Yuna drafted an early, informal succession memo favoring Daniel over Marcus before the affair began, for professional reasons unrelated to their relationship.
The entity occupying Claire's body learns of the affair by finding Daniel's phone, at a specific point in the season, not through anyone's admission.
Yuna is the only character besides Mei who begins to suspect 'Claire' has changed, though she initially misreads it as Claire having learned of the affair and planning retaliation.

episodes.intro_note: Two mirrors in, the entity wearing Claire's face has learned exactly how far Claire's trapped awareness reaches — and how careless the family's watching, worrying, and hiding have all made them. Now she starts spending what she's learned.

sections.title: Coffee, Watched
sections.what: Claire watches Daniel over coffee with unsettling precision, needling him gently about being distracted, quietly testing how much control she already holds.

sections.title: By Lamplight
sections.what: Mei rereads her grandmother's notebook by lamplight, cross-checking dates against Claire's recent strangeness, and decides she can no longer stay silent about it.

sections.title: Between Meetings
sections.what: Mei corners Daniel between meetings to warn him something is deeply wrong with Claire; he brushes her off, too distracted and guilty to truly listen.

sections.title: Another Contract
sections.what: Marcus meets his shell-company contact again, quietly rerouting another Mercer contract, still certain he'll need the insurance once Aiko eventually names Daniel sole heir.

sections.title: Tea and Concern
sections.what: Over tea, Claire gently frames Mei's talk of grandmother's stories as stress and grief, and Aiko — worried, well-meaning — quietly begins to agree.

sections.title: Among the Wrecks
sections.what: Alone among the crated wrecks, Claire corners Daniel and calmly lays out what she knows about Yuna, promising silence only if he obeys her completely.

sections.title: A Kind Excuse
sections.what: Shaken and cornered, Daniel meets Mei for a drink and deflects her warnings about Claire, gently suggesting grief over their grandmother has clouded her judgment.

sections.title: Wrong Guess
sections.what: Yuna finds Daniel rattled and evasive after work, senses something new has shifted in Claire, and wrongly assumes Claire has finally learned about them.

sections.title: Above the Bay
sections.what: Alone above the bay, Mei replays Daniel's dismissal, hurt but unwilling to let it go, and quietly resolves to find proof no one can dismiss.

sections.title: The Itinerary
sections.what: Daniel hands over the shipping itinerary entity-Claire demanded without asking why; she thanks him with a warmth that makes the compliance feel worse than a threat would.

sections.title: A Long Look
sections.what: Aiko notices another shipment routed oddly on paper, says nothing to Marcus, and watches him a beat too long before excusing herself from the office.

sections.title: Deflected
sections.what: Mei tries to warn Daniel that Claire isn't herself; he deflects, gently repeating concern about Mei's stress, the doubt the entity planted already doing its work.

sections.title: Tea and a Forgotten Scarf
sections.what: Aiko invites Mei to the pavilion for tea, an unusual overture; Mei senses kindness underneath but leaves her scarf behind, giving her a reason to return later.

sections.title: Wrong Kind of Calm
sections.what: Yuna warns Daniel that Claire's calm about the leverage doesn't fit her; Daniel insists it's guilt from the affair itself, unable to see what's really different.

sections.title: Moving Up the Timeline
sections.what: Marcus double-checks the shell company's paperwork alone on the dock, spooked by Aiko's stare earlier, and decides to move the next reroute sooner than planned.

sections.title: The Voice in the Rain
sections.what: Storm rain streaks the pavilion window; alone retrieving her scarf, Mei hears her sister's real voice break through the glass, screaming to be heard before it cuts out.

sections.title: No Longer Silent
sections.what: Shaking, Mei matches the voice to her grandmother's exact warning and decides, alone in the dark pavilion, that she will no longer stay silent about Claire.

sections.title: A Ripple She Can't Place
sections.what: Alone before the bedroom mirror, entity-Claire feels a ripple she can't place, presses her palm to the glass, and for the first time looks uncertain.

episodes.outro_note: Two people who don't know each other's reasons are now watching the same house from different angles — one certain 'Claire' is planning revenge, one certain something has already been signed away behind his back. Neither has said a word out loud. That won't last much longer.

sections.title: Before the Coffee's Cold
sections.what: Over breakfast, 'Claire' mentions a detail about Daniel's schedule she shouldn't know, watching him flinch; he says nothing, unsettled by how easily she reads him now.

sections.title: The Notebook Again
sections.what: Mei rereads her grandmother's letters and the storm-voice notes, weighing who might actually believe her instead of dismissing it as grief.

sections.title: Not This Again
sections.what: Mei tries to warn Daniel that Claire isn't herself; he cites stress and their mother's health, gently shutting her down without truly hearing her.

sections.title: A Date, Exactly
sections.what: 'Claire' corners Yuna and cites a precise date and message from the affair, calm and surgical, nothing like the wounded wife Yuna braced for.

sections.title: What Claire Never Would
sections.what: Shaken, Yuna tells Daniel she's certain Claire has been quietly building a case against them for weeks; Daniel, knowing more than he'll say, can't correct her.

sections.title: A Phrase Through the Wall
sections.what: Passing the Glass Boardroom, Marcus overhears Aiko on the phone say 'the new document' before she notices him and abruptly ends the call.

sections.title: Fishing
sections.what: Marcus casually presses Daniel about any changes to the company's future; Daniel, genuinely in the dark, gives answers that only deepen Marcus's suspicion.

sections.title: Watching Through the Rain-Glass
sections.what: In the Tea Pavilion's window glass, the trapped Claire senses Mei's resolve and Yuna's fear rising, straining toward a voice that won't come again tonight.

sections.title: Recalculating
sections.what: Alone before the vanity mirror, the entity registers both threats closing in and decides, without hurry, which one to deal with first.

case.label: The Other Woman

She wrongly concludes Claire has been quietly building a case against her for weeks, when in truth the knowledge came from a single phone discovery months ago.

case.solution: Daniel Mercer has been in a six-month affair with Yuna Park, the company's in-house counsel, born out of mutual exhaustion — Daniel drowning in his mother's health crisis and a marriage he no longer understands, Yuna caught between ambition and guilt. character_knowledge.DANIEL_MERCER: Daniel has no knowledge of his mother's health or the new document and answers Marcus's probing honestly, out of genuine ignorance rather than evasion — which only convinces Marcus that something is being hidden from him specifically.

case.solution: Aiko Mercer, diagnosed with a worsening heart condition she has hidden from everyone but her cardiologist, has secretly redrafted the company's succession plan to split ownership evenly between Daniel and Marcus Chen instead of naming Daniel sole heir as everyone assumes. Marcus does not know the new document exists; he knows only the old assumption that Daniel inherits everything, and has spent nearly a year quietly diverting smaller shipping contracts through a shell company as insurance against being frozen out, intending to use it as leverage if Aiko dies before changing anything — not realizing she already has.

sections.title: Harborview Legal, After Hours

sections.title: The Mercer House, Night
sections.what: Daniel tells 'Claire' he's confessed everything to his family, and her calm, unsurprised reply leaves him more unsettled than any anger could have.

The entity retains enough of Claire's memories and mannerisms to fool nearly everyone, failing only on small, specific behavioral tells that the real Claire would never produce.
Claire's trapped consciousness can perceive events through any reflective surface unbroken in lineage to the original mirror, but can only be glimpsed or heard under very rare conditions.
The entity's ultimate goal is to destroy or permanently seal every mirror in that connected lineage, which would make the exchange irreversible.
